coredata.dc:title: Limitation-induced fluorescence enhancement of carbon nanoparticles and their application for glucose detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Qiujun
Huang, Ting
Zhou, Jieqiong
Zeng, Yue
Wu, Cuiyan
Liu, Meiling
Li, Haitao
Zhang, Youyu
Yao, Shouzhuo
coredata.dc:description: Rational design of detection strategy is the key to high-performance fluorescence analysis. In this article, we found that the glucose-induced limitations can greatly enhance the fluorescence of functionalized carbon nanoparticles (CNPs), which are synthesized through one-step thermal pyrolysis method using phenylboronic acid derivative as the precursors. The glucose can assembly onto the surface of the CNPs to form a “shell”, limiting the surfaces' intramolecular rotation and reducing non-radiative decay, which hence resulted in enhanced fluorescence of the CNPs. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of the CNPs is nearly 70-fold enhanced, and the method has low detection limit (10 μM) and linear response in the concentration range from 50 μM to 2000 μM. Based on this interesting “target-triggered limitation-induced fluorescence enhancement” phenomenon, a simple and effective non-enzymatic fluorescence enhancement method was developed and successfully applied to the determination of glucose in spiked serum samples. This work provides new insight into the design of fluorescence-enhanced detection strategies based on the limitation-induced property.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanoparticles
Limitation-induced fluorescence enhancement
Non-enzyme detection
Glucose
Fluorometric determination
Theoretical calculation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of glucose in real samples
Glucose induced the fluorescence enhancement of CNPs
The analytical performance of the method

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21874042 , 21675051 and 21977028 ), and Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2018M640753 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of as-synthesized CNPs (inset: HRTEM image), and (B) high magnification TEM image of single CNPs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra, (B) high magnification fluorescence spectrum, and (C) UV–vis spectra of CNPs in the present of different concentration of glucose. The concentration of CNPs is 20 μg mL−1. The excitation wavelength is 320 nm. Slit width are both 5 nm. (D) FT-IR spectra of CNPs after incubation with glucose.
Molecular structures to simulate the surface of CNPs (A) before and (B) after reaction with glucose. (C, D) The corresponding rotation barrier curves.
Illustration of the detection principle of glucose. (not drawn to scale).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CNPs after adding glucose in the concentration range of 0 to 8000 μM. (B) The relationship between the fluorescence enhancement rate and the logarithm of concentration of glucose. The error bars represent standard deviation from 3 replicates and magnified by a factor of three. The concentration of CNPs is 20 μg mL−1. The excitation wavelength is 320 nm. Slit width are both 5 nm.

head.ce:title: Limitation-induced fluorescence enhancement of carbon nanoparticles and their application for glucose detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha, 410081, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Developmental Biology of Freshwater Fish, College of Life Science, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Developmental Biology of Freshwater Fish

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Developmental Biology of Freshwater Fish, College of Life Science, Hunan Normal University, Changsha, 410081, P.R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Ting Huang and the first author made equal contributions to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rational design of detection strategy is the key to high-performance fluorescence analysis. In this article, we found that the glucose-induced limitations can greatly enhance the fluorescence of functionalized carbon nanoparticles (CNPs), which are synthesized through one-step thermal pyrolysis method using phenylboronic acid derivative as the precursors. The glucose can assembly onto the surface of the CNPs to form a “shell”, limiting the surfaces' intramolecular rotation and reducing non-radiative decay, which hence resulted in enhanced fluorescence of the CNPs. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of the CNPs is nearly 70-fold enhanced, and the method has low detection limit (10 μM) and linear response in the concentration range from 50 μM to 2000 μM. Based on this interesting “target-triggered limitation-induced fluorescence enhancement” phenomenon, a simple and effective non-enzymatic fluorescence enhancement method was developed and successfully applied to the determination of glucose in spiked serum samples. This work provides new insight into the design of fluorescence-enhanced detection strategies based on the limitation-induced property.

An interesting “limitation-induced fluorescence enhancement” property of carbon nanoparticles was reported for the first time. Based on this novelty property, a new fluorescence-enhanced sensing mechanism was developed and successfully applied to the determination of glucose in human serum samples.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Biomarkers are of great importance in the fields of biology and biomedicine. As one of the most important sources of energy in all organisms, the abnormal glucose concentrations in body fluids may be the early warning signs of disease [1–3]. For example, an irregular concentration of glucose in human blood implies a biological dysfunction: a persistently high level is referred to as hyperglycemia [4]; low level is referred to as hypoglycemia [5]. Therefore, the glucose detection with highly sensitivity and selectivity is especially important in biomedicine. To this end, various methods have been successfully designed for the detection of glucose, including electrochemical methods [6,7], colorimetry [8,9], and fluorescence methods [10–12], etc. Among these methods, fluorescence methods have attracted much more attention due to its intrinsic advantages such as easy operation, high sensitivity, and widely used in biomedical and environmental sciences.

Rational design of detection method is the key for fluorescence detection with high performance. Generally, most detection strategies are based on the reaction of enzymatic oxidation of glucose by glucose oxidase [13–15], which show excellent selectivity but have some drawbacks as well. The use of the glucose oxidase increases the cost and the activity of enzyme is affected by various environmental factors. An alternative way is to create a non-enzyme strategy, in which the fluorescent probe is directly sensitive to the glucose. Based on the interaction between boronic acid groups and glucose [16–19], several non-enzyme glucose sensors have been developed. Typically, these non-enzyme glucose sensors are fabricated by two steps, including fluorescent nanomaterials and surface functionalization for the introduction of glucose recognition sites. Besides, in the case of poor water solubility of nanomaterials, additional steps are required to improve water solubility. The surface functionalization and post-modification are time-consuming processes and the performance of fluorescent nanomaterials is susceptible to the functionalization process. Moreover, most of these current non-enzyme glucose fluorescence detection methods are fluorescence “turn-off” strategy, i.e. the fluorescence of fluorophore quenched by glucose directly. Compared with fluorescence “turn-off” strategy, strategies based on fluorescence “turn-on” or enhancement is more attractive due to its higher sensitivity and better anti-interference of background signal. Therefore, the development of smart strategy for non-enzymatic glucose detection is still of great significance.

Fluorophore is another important factor affecting the performance of fluorescence detection methods. Due to their easy synthesis, stable fluorescence, high biocompatibility, good hydrophilicity and low toxicity, carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) are considered as one of the most promising fluorescent nanoparticles [20–22]. These specific characteristics make CNPs have potential applications in fluorescent biosensing, and bioimaging. Compared with semiconductor nanoparticles, one of the most attractive characteristics of CNPs is their surface controllability. Many studies have shown that the synthesized CNPs retains the chemical and photophysical properties of the precursors [23–26]. Therefore, choosing the suitable precursors is one of the key factors that determine the application performance of CNPs. For example, by using SciFinder database, the fluorescence CNPs with pK a value of 6.84 were rationally designed by Zhaohui Li et al. and applied to precise sensing of intracellular pH [23].

Herein, the boronic acid functionalization CNPs is synthesized by one-step thermal pyrolysis method using 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid and 3-aminophenylboronic acid as precursors. As expected, the synthesized CNPs were directly modified with boronic acid groups, which need no additional surface modification steps to introduce recognition sites. Based on the covalent binding between the cis-diols of glucose and boronic acid groups on the surface of CNPs, glucose can be selectively assembled with the CNPs to form a “shell” which limits the surface intramolecular rotation of the CNPs and causes the fluorescence enhancement of the CNPs (nearly 70-fold enhanced). Based on this interesting “target-triggered limitation-induced fluorescence enhancement” phenomenon, a simple but sensitive strategy was designed without the need of any enzyme and tedious surface modification process for the non-enzymatic detection of glucose. Moreover, it has been successfully applied to the detection of glucose in blood and satisfactory result was achieved.

3-carboxyphenylboronic acid and 3-aminophenylboronic acid were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China, https://www.aladdin-e.com). Other reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China, https://www.reagent.com.cn:666/ScrcBackGroup/reagent/newindex.jsp). All reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. The ultrapure water from a Millipore system (resistance ≥18.2 MΩ cm−1, https://www.merckmillipore.com) was used throughout the experiments.

UV–vis spectra were obtained by UV-2450 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Japan, https://www.shimadzu.com.cn), fluorescence spectra were measured through on an F-7000 spectrophotometer (Hitachi CO., LTD, Japan; https://www.hitachi-hightech.com). Structural analysis was performed by Nicolet Nexus 670 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrophotometer (Nicolet Instrument Inc., USA, https://www.thermofisher.com). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected on a JEOL-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL CO., LTD, Japan, https://www.jeol.co.jp). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was done with Al, K as the exciting source on Thermo Fisher Scientific K-Alpha 1063 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA, https://www.thermofisher.com). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential were measurement through Nano-ZSZetzsozer ZEN3600 (Malvern Instruments Ltd., U.K., https://malvern.cnpowder.com.cn). The fluorescence lifetimes were measured with a time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer model FLS 920 (Edinburgh Instruments LTD, U.K., https://www.edinst.com).

The CNPs were prepared through a facile thermal pyrolysis method. In brief, 0.5 g 3-carboxyphenylboronic acid and 0.3 g 3-aminophenylboronic acid were put into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and the reaction was kept in an oven at 120 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the prepared product was dialyzed (Mw 1000) against water for two days to remove the impurities.

The detection of glucose was performed at environment temperature (about 20 °C). 800 μL of CNPs (final concentration is 20 μg mL−1), 100 μL of different concentrations of glucose solution, and 100 μL of 1 g mL−1 NaOH were mixed and shaken for 5 h. Subsequently, the fluorescence spectrum of the reaction solution measured at the excitation wavelength of 320 nm. Each experiment was repeated three times. The error bars in figures represent standard deviation from 3 replicates.

A anthracene derivatives model with a negative charge were constructed to simulate the surface of CNPs [27,28]. All structures were optimized using B3LYP/6-31+G(d) method in the case of water as solvent with Gaussian 09 program package [29]. The rotation barriers of boric acid groups before and after reaction with glucose were studied by rigid scan with Gaussian 09 program package and Molclus program package [29,30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glucose in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Human serum samples were provided by Hospital of Hunan Normal University (Changsha, China). The samples were diluted 100-fold by water. Then, the following steps were similar to those of the fluorescence detection for glucose, and the test solutions were spiked with standard solutions including varied concentrations of glucose.

The morphology of CNPs was characterized by the TEM technique. As shown in Fig. 1A and B, the synthesized CNPs are mostly spherical with good dispersity. Fig. S1A displayed that the diameter for CNPs is 85 ± 18 nm. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1A, inset) shows a crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.20 nm, which is similar to the (102) diffraction planes of graphitic carbon (JCPDS 26-1076) [24]. The energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX, Fig. S1B) displays that the CNPs contain B, C, N and O four elements.

The UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectra were used to study the optical properties of the CNPs. As shown in Fig. S1C, the CNPs have a characteristic absorption band at 215 nm [31,32], and the excitation and emission of CNPs were peaked at 320 nm and 420 nm, respectively. As shown in Fig. S1D, like most other fluorescence CNPs, the synthesized CNPs show excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior, indicating that surface states might be involved in the fluorescence process [24,33].

The surface groups and chemical compositions of CNPs were investigated by FT-IR and XPS techniques. As shown in Fig. S2A, the strong band at about 3418 cm−1 corresponds to the -OH stretching vibration. The peaks at about 1383 cm−1, 1157 cm−1 and 1063 cm−1, are attributed to the stretching vibration of B-O, bending vibration of B-O-H and stretching vibration of C-B, respectively [34–38]. The FT-IR results indicate that the surfaces of CNPs are successfully functionalized with boronic acid groups, and the XPS spectra further confirmed it. The XPS survey spectra show that the CNPs contain B, C, N and O four elements (Fig. S2B,), with the atomic percentages of 2.09%, 66.45%, 3.01% and 28.45%, respectively. As shown in Fig. S2C, B1s spectrum was fitted into three peaks that correspond to the following functional groups: B-C at 190.3 eV, C-B-(OH)2 at 191.4 eV and B-O at 192.6 eV [36,37]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. S2D) were curve-fitted into four peaks with B-O at 531.4 eV, O=C-O at 532.2 eV, C-O at 533.0 eV and O=C-O at 533.7 eV [39,40]. These results reflect that the boronic acid groups were well preserved during the synthesis of CNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Glucose induced the fluorescence enhancement of CNPs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2A and B, the CNPs exhibit weak fluorescence intensity at 420 nm. However, when the glucose was introduced, the intensity enhanced significantly. The fluorescence intensity increased about 46-fold, 60-fold, and 68-fold for 0.5 mM, 1 mM and 2 mM glucose, respectively, which means that quantitative detection of glucose can be achieved by measuring the fluorescence intensity of the CNPs.

The fluorescence enhancement mechanism of boronic acid functionalization CNPs after the introducing of glucose was investigated. Based on the previous reported, boronic acid can bind with cis-diols via covalent interaction to form cyclic esters [16,17,41]. As shown in Fig. 2C, the absorption band of the CNPs changed when glucose is added, indicating that glucose may interact with the CNPs. In addition, after incubation with glucose, the characteristic peak of B-O-H at 1157 cm−1 is significantly decreased (Fig. 2D and Fig. S3), and two characteristic peaks at 2925 cm−1 and 2858 cm−1 are newly appeared (Fig. 2D), which are assigned to the anti-symmetric stretching vibration and symmetrical stretching vibration of methylene. These results indicated that glucose bind on the surface of CNPs and form complexes with the boronic acid groups of CNPs.

The TEM image was further confirmed it. After adding glucose to the CNPs solution, a low contrast “shell” can be found on the surface of CNPs (Fig. S4A and S4B). This may due to the large amount of boronic acid groups on the surface of CNPs anchoring the glucose in the solution via the affinity between boronic acid groups and glucose, thereby forming a “shell” around the CNPs. Unlike other boronic acid functionalized nanoparticles, the added glucose can covalently bridge the nanoparticles to form large assemblies and cause fluorescence quenching of nanoparticles [34,38,42]. In this paper, no further assembly between CNPs was occurred. This may be due to the formation of negatively charged boronate complex after the interaction between glucose and boronic acid group (Fig. S5). Thus, the large electrostatic repulsion prevents the cross-linking and aggregation of the glucose assembled CNPs. The formation of glucose “shell” was also examined by dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement (Fig. S6A). The hydrodynamic size of CNPs was significantly increased after the addition of glucose, indicating the formation of glucose “shell”. The different diameters measured by TEM and DLS are due to different surface states of the sample under the different measurement conditions [43].

We speculate that the glucose “shell” has a passivating effect on the CNPs, which can hamper the surfaces' intramolecular rotations of the boronic acid groups of CNPs and reduce non-radiative decay, resulting in enhanced fluorescence of the CNPs (i.e. limitation-induced fluorescence enhancement) [44]. As previously reported, increasing the viscosity of solvent can also hamper the rotations of the boronic acid groups of CNPs [45]. Since different ratios of glycerol-water mixtures can produce different viscosities, we further measured the fluorescence of CNPs in different ratios of glycerol-water mixtures. As shown in Fig. S6B, the fluorescence of the CNPs increased about 46-fold as the glycerol fraction increased to 80%. This suggests that reducing the rate of rotation leads to enhanced fluorescence. Furthermore, the time-resolved fluorescence spectra were also used to reveal the fluorescence enhancement mechanism of CNPs. As shown in Fig. S6C and 6D, the fluorescence lifetime of CNPs is 10.27 ± 0.03 ns when fit with a single exponential function. After the addition of glucose, the lifetime of CNPs enhanced to 10.76 ± 0.03 ns. The enhanced lifetime and fluorescence may be due to the fact that the intramolecular rotation is limited, and the non-radiative decay is reduced after reaction with glucose. Moreover, in order to further provide insight into the restriction of the intramolecular rotations of the boronic acid groups after the formation of glucose “shell”, theoretical calculations based on density functional theory (DFT) were also carried out. A anthracene derivatives model were constructed to simulate the surface of CNPs [27,28]. The geometry was optimized, and the results were shown in Fig. 3A and B. The rotation barrier of boric acid groups before and after reaction with glucose was studied by rigid scan (Supplementary Movies 1 and 2). As shown in Fig. 3C and D, the rotation barrier of boric acid groups increased significantly compared with the original CNPs (from 0.0152 Eh to 0.1743 Eh) after reaction with glucose, indicating that the glucose can hamper the rotations of the boronic acid groups of CNPs.

We speculate that the glucose “shell” has a passivating effect on the CNPs, which can hamper the surfaces' intramolecular rotations of the boronic acid groups of CNPs and reduce non-radiative decay, resulting in enhanced fluorescence of the CNPs (i.e. limitation-induced fluorescence enhancement) [44]. As previously reported, increasing the viscosity of solvent can also hamper the rotations of the boronic acid groups of CNPs [45]. Since different ratios of glycerol-water mixtures can produce different viscosities, we further measured the fluorescence of CNPs in different ratios of glycerol-water mixtures. As shown in Fig. S6B, the fluorescence of the CNPs increased about 46-fold as the glycerol fraction increased to 80%. This suggests that reducing the rate of rotation leads to enhanced fluorescence. Furthermore, the time-resolved fluorescence spectra were also used to reveal the fluorescence enhancement mechanism of CNPs. As shown in Fig. S6C and 6D, the fluorescence lifetime of CNPs is 10.27 ± 0.03 ns when fit with a single exponential function. After the addition of glucose, the lifetime of CNPs enhanced to 10.76 ± 0.03 ns. The enhanced lifetime and fluorescence may be due to the fact that the intramolecular rotation is limited, and the non-radiative decay is reduced after reaction with glucose. Moreover, in order to further provide insight into the restriction of the intramolecular rotations of the boronic acid groups after the formation of glucose “shell”, theoretical calculations based on density functional theory (DFT) were also carried out. A anthracene derivatives model were constructed to simulate the surface of CNPs [27,28]. The geometry was optimized, and the results were shown in Fig. 3A and B. The rotation barrier of boric acid groups before and after reaction with glucose was studied by rigid scan (Supplementary Movies 1 and 2). As shown in Fig. 3C and D, the rotation barrier of boric acid groups increased significantly compared with the original CNPs (from 0.0152 Eh to 0.1743 Eh) after reaction with glucose, indicating that the glucose can hamper the rotations of the boronic acid groups of CNPs.

Based on the above results, the proposed sensing mechanism is depicted in Fig. 4 : the synthesized CNPs with large amount of boronic acid groups on the surface, which can bind to glucose and form a glucose “shell” on the surface of CNPs; the glucose “shell” limits the surfaces' intramolecular rotation of CNPs and blocks the nonradiative decay while populates the radiative decay, thus making the fluorescence center highly emissive, i.e. enhancing the fluorescence of CNPs [41,46–48]. Therefore, a fluorescence enhancement sensing platform for non-enzymatic fluorescence detection of glucose was constructed.

As mentioned above, the glucose “shell” will directly affect the sensitivity of the proposed method, while the concentration of CNPs, pH and incubation time are the key factors affecting the formation of glucose “shell”. Therefore, the concentration of CNPs, pH and incubation time should be optimized to improve the detection sensitivity. The effect of the concentration of CNPs was studied firstly, and the results are shown in Fig. S7A. It is clearly seen that the fluorescence enhancement rate F/F 0 (F and F 0 correspond to the fluorescence intensity of the CNPs in the presence and absence of glucose, respectively) reaches the highest value at the concentration of CNPs at 20 μg mL−1. This may be due to the higher the concentration of CNPs, the lower the proportion of CNPs that can be form a glucose “shell”. Thus, 20 μg mL−1 of CNPs was chosen as the optimum concentration. As shown in Fig. S7B, the fluorescence of CNPs is enhanced significantly under alkaline conditions compared with acidic or neutral conditions. Since the reaction of boronic acid groups with glucose is a release proton reaction, thus, alkaline condition is favorable [49]. It is observed that the fluorescence enhancement rate reaching the maximum when the reaction time was 5 h, as shown in Fig. S7C. In this work, pH 14 and incubation for 5 h were selected as the optimum conditions. However, it should be noted that under the mild conditions (such as in physiological pH, incubation 1 h), the fluorescence response is also very sensitive, with an intensity about 2.9-fold higher than that in the absence of glucose. Therefore, it is also feasible to select other conditions according to the actual analyte situation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The analytical performance of the method
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions, the analytical performances were studied with the addition of different concentrations of glucose. As shown in Fig. 5A, the fluorescence intensity of the CNPs increased significantly with the concentration of glucose increasing, and a maximum 70-fold enhancement can be achieved when the concentration of glucose is 8 mM. Simultaneously, a good liner relationship is observed between the fluorescence enhancement rate and the logarithm of the concentration of glucose in the range from 50 to 2000 μM (Fig. 5B). The corresponding correlation equation is F/F 0 = 40.9516–65.9176[log(glucose)] with correlation coefficient (R 2) of 0.9818. The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated as 10 μM, which covers the physiological concentration of glucose in serum, and comparable to or better than those of most previous boronic acid-based detection methods (Table S1).

The potential interfering substances including various amino acids, common ions and saccharides were investigated. As shown in Fig. S8, the amino acids and common ions have no effects on the fluorescence of CNPs, which can be attributed to the lack of reaction sites on the surface of CNPs. The selectivity is attributed to the high interaction of boronic acid groups on the surface of CNPs and cis-diols of glucose. It was also found that the glucose analogues such as fructose, galactose, mannose and lactose exhibited similar fluorescence enhancement, which was a reasonable result of their similar binding affinity to the glucose. In the blood, fasting blood glucose in healthy individuals (3.9–6.1 mM) is much higher than fructose, galactose, mannose and lactose (all <0.1 mM) [41]. Therefore, the interference from fructose, galactose, mannose and lactose can be ignored in practical applications.

The potential applicability of this method was verified by a standard addition method. Certain concentration of glucose standard solutions was directly spiked into the human serum samples before dilution. As shown in Table S2, this method has satisfactory recoveries in the ranges from 92% to 105%, implying the potential application of this method in the detection of glucose in real samples.

In summary, a novel “limitation-induced fluorescence enhancement” property has been reported. Experimental and theoretical calculations showed that limits the surfaces' intramolecular rotation of CNPs can blocks the nonradiative decay, while populates the radiative decay, thus making the fluorescence center highly emissive, i.e. enhancing the fluorescence of CNPs. Furthermore, based on the covalent binding between the glucose and boronic acid groups, this novel property has been successfully applied to the determination of glucose in human serum sample. There is no doubt that our research provides new guidelines for building high performance detection method and we believe this new sensing mechanism will be widely used in biosensing in the future.

The following are the supplementary data related to this article.
Supplementary Movie 1
The rotation barrier of CNPs before reaction with glucose.
Supplementary Movie 1
Supplementary Movie 2
The rotation barrier of CNPs after reaction with glucose.
Supplementary Movie 2
Supplementary material
Image 1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2020.118893.

Qiujun Lu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition. Ting Huang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis. Jieqiong Zhou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis. Yue Zeng: Data curation, Formal analysis. Cuiyan Wu: Formal analysis. Meiling Liu: Resources, Writing - review & editing. Haitao Li: Resources, Writing - review & editing. Youyu Zhang: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Shouzhuo Yao: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874042, 21675051 and 21977028), and Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018M640753).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multiwalled carbon nanotubes coated with cobalt(II) sulfide nanoparticles for electrochemical sensing of glucose via direct electron transfer to glucose oxidase

Acta, 187 (2020) 80. doi:10.1007/s00604-019-4047-8.
